title: Custom Metal Shaders via torch.mps.compile_shader
description: Write custom Metal Shading Language (MSL) kernels and dispatch them from PyTorch. Learn the Apple GPU's threadgroup model, simdgroup intrinsics, and how torch.mps.compile_shader bridges Python and Metal.

sections.title: Why Custom Kernels?
sections.content:
Coming soon — this module will cover when and why you'd reach for a custom Metal Shading Language (MSL) kernel instead of stock PyTorch ops.

<!-- TODO:
  - Motivate: fused softmax, fused GELU, custom attention; cases where the eager PyTorch op chain is memory-bound and a single fused kernel halves wall time.
  - Show a real measurement: e.g. fused softmax+dropout vs. composed ops on M2 Max.
  - Compare to CUDA's analogous story (numba.cuda / Triton) so readers coming from the CUDA course have a hook.
  - Link to torch.mps.compile_shader (PyTorch 2.5+) docs and the older MetalKernel extension API.
-->

sections.title: Metal Shading Language Basics
sections.content:
Coming soon — a primer on Metal Shading Language (MSL): kernel functions, threadgroups, threadgroup memory, SIMD-group intrinsics.

<!-- TODO:
  - Map MSL concepts to CUDA equivalents: kernel = __global__, threadgroup = block, simdgroup = warp, threadgroup_barrier(metal::mem_flags::mem_threadgroup) = __syncthreads().
  - Worked example kernel: vector add in MSL, dispatched via torch.mps.compile_shader.
  - simd_shuffle, simdgroup_matrix (Apple's tensor-core analogue on M3+/M4).
  - Debugging with Metal Frame Capture in Xcode.
